fields.title: Traditional Irish Stew
fields.short_description: A rustic, slow-simmered one-pot of mutton (or lamb), root vegetables and pearl barley — Ireland's defining country supper, layered into a heavy casserole and cooked low until everything melts together.
fields.ingredients:
900 g (2 lb) mutton or lamb (scrag end and neck), chopped into bite-sized pieces
2 tbsp plain (all-purpose) flour
½ tsp salt, plus more to taste
½ tsp freshly ground black pepper
3 onions, sliced
4 carrots, sliced
2 leeks, sliced
5 potatoes, thickly sliced
1 tbsp pearl barley
1 tsp finely chopped fresh thyme leaves
1 tbsp finely chopped fresh parsley
900 ml (1½ pints / 3¾ cups) lamb or mutton stock
1 tbsp butter, melted
fields.method:
1. Trim the excess fat from the meat and reserve it. In a heavy flameproof casserole over medium heat, render the trimmed fat until it releases its juices. Lift the cracklings out with a slotted spoon and discard, leaving the rendered fat behind.

2. Combine the flour with half the salt and pepper, then toss the meat in the seasoned flour until evenly coated. Add the meat to the casserole and brown on all sides over medium-high heat, working in batches if needed so the pan never crowds. Lift out about three-quarters of the meat with a slotted spoon, leaving a thin layer at the bottom of the dish.

3. Begin layering: a quarter of the onions, a quarter of the carrots, a quarter of the leeks, a pinch of salt and pepper, then a quarter of the potatoes. Repeat the layers three more times, returning the rest of the meat as you go. Before the final layer of potatoes, scatter in the pearl barley and thyme.

4. Stir the parsley into the stock and pour it gently over the stew. Spoon the melted butter over the top.

5. Bring to a boil for 3 minutes and skim off any scum from the surface. Reduce the heat to low, cover tightly, and simmer for about 2 hours, until the meat is fork-tender and the vegetables have melted into a thick, savoury gravy.

6. Taste, adjust the seasoning, and serve in deep bowls — traditionally with parsley dumplings and a hunk of soda bread on the side.
fields.tips:
• Mutton gives the deepest flavour but is harder to find; a good lamb shoulder is the most common modern substitute. Avoid lean cuts — you want connective tissue for richness.
• Make a day ahead for the best flavour. Cool, refrigerate, and gently reheat the next day; the gravy thickens beautifully.
• Pearl barley is optional but very traditional — it thickens the broth and adds a nutty bite.
• If you cannot find leeks, double the onions instead.
fields.irish_context: Irish stew is, by long tradition, a peasant dish of the rural west: slow-cooked mutton from older animals, paired with whatever root vegetables and barley were laid up in the larder. The original recipe was strictly mutton, onions, potatoes and water — a single-pot supper hung over a peat fire all afternoon. Carrots, leeks and herbs are nineteenth- and twentieth-century additions that took hold once trade and agriculture diversified. You will still find good-natured argument in pubs from Cork to Donegal over whether 'real' Irish stew should contain carrots at all.
fields.allergens: Contains dairy (butter). Naturally gluten-free if the flour for coating the meat is omitted or replaced with a gluten-free blend. Suitable for nut-, egg- and shellfish-free diets.
fields.source_title: Traditional Irish Cooking: The Fare of Old Ireland and its History
fields.source_note: Garnet Publishing, 2008. ISBN 978-1-85964-155-2 (page 37). Adapted in CulinEire's voice.
